A system is asked to improve itself by feeding its own “discoveries” back into the draw pool. On the surface, this is efficient: useful patterns found early become ingredients for later search. But the same mechanism also gives false positives a way to persist. If an early mistake is judged promising, it is not merely retained; it becomes more likely to be sampled again. Search then narrows around whatever was mistakenly favored. The result is not open-ended improvement but attraction toward brittle local minima.

To prevent that, the system would need to judge its own outputs well enough to distinguish genuine progress from seductive error. But that requirement is the dangerous point. Any bug in self-evaluation is no longer a single bug affecting one decision; it becomes a multiplier. Once the evaluator slightly overvalues some trait, recursive reuse spreads that bias through subsequent generations. The stronger the reliance on self-judgment, the stronger the amplification of evaluator flaws.

Suppose the system uses grammar and coherence as early filters, because they are cheap and often correlate with acceptable outputs. This dramatically collapses the search space. But the collapse has direction: it favors what already looks well-formed and familiar. Rare, strange constructions—the ones most likely to contain explanatory novelty precisely because they do not resemble standard outputs—are filtered out early. So even if the recursive loop is stable, it is stabilized around cliché. The system becomes better at producing what passes its filters, not necessarily what is true or insightful.

Now place the remaining premises inside that world. “Producing a truce amazed those who fainted when war was abolished.” The sentence is coherent enough to survive a grammar filter, yet semantically it exposes a mismatch in evaluative framing. If war was abolished, a truce is conceptually redundant or mis-scaled; amazement and fainting signal reactions that are not proportionate to the underlying state. This is what evaluator failure looks like in miniature: agents respond strongly to a derivative artifact while missing the more fundamental change. A self-improving system with flawed judgment could likewise reward superficial markers of resolution while failing to recognize the underlying elimination of the problem.

“Hurray for the profoundly severe blueberry donation” is also grammatical and coherent enough to pass shallow filters while remaining evaluatively unstable. “Hurray” marks approval; “profoundly severe” marks gravity or harshness; “blueberry donation” appears benign or arbitrary. The phrase assembles signals that fit local criteria without resolving into a trustworthy global judgment. That is exactly the kind of output a recursively self-filtering system might preserve: polished, classifiable, and internally brittle.

So the collision of premises implies a world where recursive self-improvement is most vulnerable at the evaluator, where early linguistic filters select for surface legitimacy, and where contamination compounds because plausible nonsense can survive long enough to become training material. In such a world, the path to self-improvement and the path to self-poisoning are structurally similar.
